title: Body Surface Area (BSA) Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Body Surface Area (BSA) – Du Bois, Mosteller & Haycock
description: Body surface area (BSA) calculator using Du Bois, Mosteller & Haycock formulas. Calculate BSA for chemotherapy dosing, cardiac index & medical applications.
seoKeywords: how to calculate body surface area
intro: **Body Surface Area (BSA)** is used in medicine to calculate **chemotherapy doses**, determine **cardiac index**, and adjust medication dosing. Measured in **m²** (square meters). This calculator uses the 3 most widely accepted formulas: **Du Bois (1916)**, **Mosteller (1987)**, and **Haycock (1978)**. The average adult has approximately **1.7 m²** of body surface area.
keyTakeaway: **Mosteller** (the simplest formula): `BSA = √(weight × height / 3600)`. A 75 kg adult who is 175 cm tall has a BSA of approximately **1.91 m²**. This is essential for calculating chemotherapy doses in mg/m².
useCases: You need to calculate your BSA for a medical dose (chemotherapy, antibiotics).
You're a healthcare professional adjusting medication based on body surface area.
You're calculating your cardiac index (cardiac output / BSA).
You're evaluating renal function corrected for body surface area.
You want to compare the 3 most commonly used formulas.

dataUpdate.notes: Du Bois 1916, Mosteller 1987, Haycock 1978. Medical standards.

example.title: Example: 75 kg adult, 175 cm tall
example.result: Body surface area: **~1.91 m²** (all 3 formulas yield very similar results).

explanation:
## The 3 Formulas

### Mosteller (1987) — Most widely used today
```
BSA (m²) = √(weight(kg) × height(cm) / 3600)
```
Preferred in oncology for its simplicity.

### Du Bois & Du Bois (1916) — The original
```
BSA = 0.007184 × weight^0.425 × height(cm)^0.725
```
Based on only 9 subjects but surprisingly accurate.

### Haycock (1978) — Preferred in pediatrics
```
BSA = 0.024265 × weight^0.5378 × height(cm)^0.3964
```
Validated with a larger sample including children.

## Reference values

| Group | Average BSA (m²) |
|---|---|
| Newborn | 0.25 |
| 10-year-old child | 1.14 |
| Adult female | 1.60–1.80 |
| Adult male | 1.80–2.00 |
| Average adult | 1.73 |

## Medical applications

- **Chemotherapy**: dosing in mg/m². Example: docetaxel 75 mg/m².
- **Cardiac index**: cardiac output (L/min) / BSA. Normal: 2.5–4.0 L/min/m².
- **Corrected glomerular filtration rate**: GFR is reported per 1.73 m².
- **Antibiotic dosing**: vancomycin, aminoglycosides.
- **Transplantation**: donor/recipient size compatibility.

## Differences between formulas

The 3 formulas yield very similar results for normal-weight adults (difference <2%). Larger differences appear at the **extremes**: severe obesity, cachexia, or very small children.

faq.q: Why does body surface area matter in medicine?
faq.a: Because the **dosage of many medications** is adjusted based on m² of body surface area, not weight alone. This is more accurate because body surface area correlates better with how the drug is distributed in the body.
For adults, all 3 formulas are roughly equivalent. For **children**, Haycock is preferred. For **obesity**, none is ideal — some clinics use adjusted weight instead.

faq.q: How is body surface area used in chemotherapy?
faq.a: Chemotherapy doses are prescribed in **mg/m²**. For example, if a drug is dosed at 75 mg/m² and your BSA is 1.9 m², your dose is 75 × 1.9 = **142.5 mg**.

faq.q: Does body surface area change when you lose weight?
faq.a: Yes. If you lose 10 kg, your BSA may decrease by ~0.1 m². In chemotherapy, BSA is **recalculated before each treatment cycle**.

faq.q: What is cardiac index and what are normal values?
faq.a: Cardiac index is cardiac output (liters of blood pumped per minute) divided by your BSA. Normal range: **2.5–4.0 L/min/m²**. It's measured with an echocardiogram.

faq.q: Can you calculate body surface area for babies and children?
faq.a: Yes. The Haycock formula is most accurate for newborns and children. For a 3 kg baby who is 50 cm tall: BSA ≈ **0.20 m²**.

faq.q: What is a normal body surface area for adults?
faq.a: Average adult BSA is about **1.73 m²** (range 1.6–2.0 m²). Adult women typically have 1.6–1.8 m², while men are usually 1.8–2.0 m².

faq.q: Why was the Du Bois formula based on so few people?
faq.a: The original 1916 Du Bois formula was developed with only 9 subjects, but remarkably, it remains accurate. Modern studies have validated it against larger populations.

faq.q: Can I use a body composition scale to measure body surface area?
faq.a: No. Bioimpedance scales measure weight and estimate body composition, but they don't calculate surface area. You need weight + height and a BSA formula.

faq.q: How often should BSA be recalculated for chemotherapy patients?
faq.a: BSA should be **recalculated before each chemotherapy cycle**, as it changes with weight changes. Even small weight losses can affect dosing accuracy.
